headline: MILK CRASHES 5% AS GRAINS STAY STEADY
subheadline: Dairy producers face reality check while corn and beans hold pattern through early planting.
lead: Class III milk crashed to $17.34 overnight, down a brutal 5.1% in a stark reminder of dairy's volatility problem we discussed yesterday. Meanwhile, corn barely budged at $4.51 per bushel and soybeans held $11.65 as planting season begins in earnest across the Corn Belt.
teaser: Dairy's overnight crash overshadows steady grain trade as planting season accelerates.

one_number.context: Down 5.1% overnight in a brutal reminder that dairy margins can evaporate faster than morning dew. This drop wipes out weeks of gains and puts many operations back underwater on feed costs.

sections.title: Grains Hold Steady
sections.body: Corn at $4.51 and soybeans at $11.65 are barely moving as farmers shift focus from price boards to field conditions. Weather is king now — every planting day matters with tight old-crop supplies still sitting in bins. Chicago wheat managed a 1% gain to $6.08, but that's more technical than fundamental. The real action is happening in tractors, not trading pits.
sections.bottom_line: Grains treading water as planting takes priority over price discovery.
sections.farmer_action: Keep weather radars open and pricing fingers ready — any significant rain threats could spark quick rallies to move remaining old crop.

sections.title: Dairy Reality Check
sections.body: Class III milk's 5.1% overnight plunge to $17.34 is exactly why dairy producers live with constant margin anxiety. Volatility this extreme makes planning nearly impossible when feed costs stay sticky but milk prices swing like a pendulum. Live cattle held steady at $251.22, but feeder cattle slipped 0.9% to $372.07 as the market digests these premium levels.
sections.bottom_line: Dairy's overnight crash reminds everyone why this business isn't for the faint-hearted.
sections.farmer_action: Dairy producers should reassess risk management strategies immediately — this volatility demands active hedging, not hope.

sections.title: Energy Inputs Slip
sections.body: WTI crude dropped 3.5% to $88.49 overnight, providing some diesel relief as planting season ramps up. Natural gas gained 0.5% to $2.60, but remains near annual lows. Fuel cost relief comes at the perfect time for spring fieldwork, though planting demand typically pushes regional diesel prices higher regardless of crude moves.
sections.bottom_line: Crude's drop offers fuel relief just as tractors fire up across the Midwest.
sections.farmer_action: Lock remaining diesel needs this week — planting season demand concentration can override broader crude weakness.

sections.title: Macro Backdrop
sections.body: The S&P 500 gained 0.8% to $7,022.95, hitting fresh all-time highs while the dollar index held flat at $100.00. Risk appetite remains strong despite commodity volatility, suggesting broader economic confidence. Gold slipped 0.2% to $4,838 as investors favor growth assets over safe havens.
sections.bottom_line: Broader markets stay bullish while agricultural volatility remains sector-specific.
sections.farmer_action: Focus on operational fundamentals rather than macro trades — your weather matters more than Wall Street right now.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Planting Speed Actually Matters
the_more_you_know.body: Every day of delayed corn planting after the optimal window costs roughly 1 bushel per acre in yield potential. It's not just old wives' tales — university data consistently shows this penalty. The corn plant's genetics are programmed for specific day-length triggers, and late planting throws off the entire reproductive cycle. This is why April weather can move markets more than USDA reports during planting season.

watch_list.desc: Any significant rain threats during optimal planting window
Planting progress reports from major corn states
Class III milk recovery potential after overnight crash

daily_quote.text: Agriculture not only gives riches to a nation, but the only riches she can call her own.

source_summary: CME Group, CBOT, NYMEX settlements and overnight electronic trading
